title: Attention-based optimizer for symmetry finding
authors: Shreya Banerjee, Vinodh Raj Rajagopal Muthu, Charlie Nation, Rick P. A. Simon, Francesco Martini, Alessandro Ricottone, Federico Cerisola, Luca Dellantonio
abstract: Finding symmetries is crucial for understanding physical models. In this work, we present an optimization framework that searches Pauli symmetries of Hamiltonians, merging the fields of machine learning with automated symmetry finding. Built on a Set-Transformer architecture, our framework uses self-attention to encode the pairwise and higher-order correlations among the Pauli-Strings. The relations are then decoded as a candidate, which is further optimized with a custom commutation-based objective, and mapped to a symmetry of the input Hamiltonian. We apply our method to random Pauli Hamiltonians, periodic one and two dimensional transverse-field Ising model and the Toric code. We show that for physical Hamiltonians (Ising and Toric), our framework succeeds with near-deterministic probability while providing substantial advantage compared to state-of-the-art strategies. For random Pauli Hamiltonians, we estimate the required computational resources, specifically the number of parallel starts and the number of GPUs, to find a symmetry with high success probability under fixed design specifications.
summary: This paper introduces an attention-based optimization framework that uses a Set-Transformer to automatically discover Pauli symmetries of quantum Hamiltonians. By minimizing a loss function derived from the commutation relation, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance on physical models like the Ising and Toric codes. This represents a significant integration of machine learning into the field of quantum many-body physics.
main_problem: The core problem is to automatically find the Pauli symmetries of a given quantum Hamiltonian, merging automated symmetry detection with machine learning techniques.
main_result: The proposed attention-based optimizer successfully finds symmetries for physical Hamiltonians (Ising, Toric code) with near-deterministic probability, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art deterministic methods.
method: The framework uses a Set-Transformer architecture to encode correlations among Pauli-Strings, optimizing a loss function based on the commutation relation between the Hamiltonian and the candidate symmetry.
model_or_system: The method is applied to various quantum systems, including random Pauli Hamiltonians, the transverse-field Ising model (1D and 2D), and the Toric code. The Hamiltonian is represented in a tableau form.
key_observables: Commutation loss ($	ext{C}_{	ext{com}}$), failure probability ($p_f$), required computational resources (parallel starts, GPUs), and the resulting symmetry vector $S_p$.
important_parameters: The rank ($R$) of the Hamiltonian, the system size ($n_q$), and regularization coefficients ($\alpha_{com}, \alpha_{zp}, \alpha_{bin}, \alpha_{lin}$).
main_assumptions: The use of $\sin^2( rac{\pi}{2} x)$ as a differentiable proxy for modulo 2 arithmetic introduces a highly multi-modal loss landscape, requiring multiple optimization starts.
figures_summary: Figures benchmark the optimizer against deterministic methods on random and physical Hamiltonians, showing speedups in time and iterations required to find symmetries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ML framework (Set-Transformer, loss function, ACE module) and then validates it by benchmarking performance across random, 1D Ising, and 2D Ising/Toric code Hamiltonians, comparing results against established deterministic algorithms.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the structure of quantum Hamiltonians by using deep learning to discover fundamental symmetries, which is crucial for simplifying and understanding complex many-body physics models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic diagram for the attention-based optimization framework to find a Pauli symmetry Sp of the input Hamiltonian H. H is first embedded into its tableau representation Ht [49, 50] in the input embedding layer, and then is passed through a linear row-wise projection layer [51, 52]. The underlying machine-learning model is a Set-Transformer [51, 53] (light-blue box with dashed border in left), consisting of Encoder-Decoder stacks and an activation module. The encoder (light-orange) contains stacked Set Attention Blocks (SAB) [51] (two in the figure). The Decoder (dark green) includes a Pooling Multi-head Attention layer (PMA) [54], followed by a SAB, layer-norm [55], and a linear...

FIG. 2: (a): Benchmark on random Hamiltonians with 10 qubits and increasing R. a(1) shows the average (light- green) and minimum (dark-green) times taken to find a symmetry. a(2) showcases the performance of the ACE module. We plot the average (light-green) and minimum (dark-green) contexts N needed to find a symmetry, rescaled by the full context (red dashed line) 2R. The orange dashed line reports the initial number of Pauli-Strings ≈R2. a(3) presents the probability of failure pf (red) of the attention-based framework and the number of parallel starts ns (green stars) required by the optimizer to achieve a 90% success rate, as well as the number of GPUs nGPU (green triangles) to enable...
